figures.caption: Figure 9. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the ZB medium task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.

Figure 10. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the ZB hard task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.
Figure 7. Normalized confusion matrix of the classifier from pooled out-of-fold predictions under 5-fold stratified cross-validation.

Figure 14. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the SC easy task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.
Table 1. Performance comparison on the DR dataset across three tasks. Refer to Tables 7 and 8 for all datasets.

Figure 13. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the DR hard task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.
Table 7. Performance comparison on easy (interpolation) and medium (extrapolation) tasks across three datasets.

Figure 19. PCA visualization of the ground truth samples and model predictions on SC data for easy (top row), medium (middle row), and hard (bottom row) tasks. The gray points represent the training data.

Figure 20. Learned latent temporal functions for the SC dataset under the medium task. The smooth trajectories and varying uncertainty bands illustrate the model’s ability to capture both the population-level trends and the time-varying noise across latent dimensions.

Figure 18. PCA visualization of the ground truth samples and model predictions on DR data for easy (top row), medium (middle row), and hard (bottom row) tasks. The gray points represent the training data.

Figure 17. PCA visualization of the ground truth samples and model predictions on ZB data for easy (top row), medium (middle row), and hard (bottom row) tasks. The gray points represent the training data.
Figure 5. Sensitivity analysis to key hyperparameters on the SC dataset. Results are shown for medium (top) and hard (bottom) tasks using the validation splits from hyperparameter tuning.
Table 8. Performance comparison on hard tasks (interpolation + extrapolation) across three datasets.

Figure 12. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the DR medium task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.
Figure 2. Visual comparison of predicted and observed cell distributions on the ZB dataset.

Figure 3. Effect of perturbing different numbers of DE genes of PSM and Hindbrain cells on the predicted fraction of cells classified into each type at the last time point of the ZB dataset. The dashed horizontal line marks the observed cell type ratio.
Figure 6. PCA visualization of the most DE genes of PSM and Hindbrain cells identified from the final time point of the ZB dataset.

Figure 16. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the SC hard task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.

Figure 11. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the DR easy task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.

Figure 8. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the ZB easy task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.

Figure 4. Inferred trajectories under gene and cell type-specific perturbations. Left: Inferred trajectories resulting from perturbing only the most DE gene of PSM and Hindbrain cells. The original model’s mean trajectory steered toward the Hindbrain cluster by scaling up the SOX19A gene, or toward the PSM cluster by scaling up the HES6 gene. Right: Inferred trajectories where the optimization target is defined by the distribution of the specific cell types themselves, rather than scaling specific genes.

Figure 15. Comparison of predicted and observed distributions across models on test time points of the SC medium task. The black contours, ρ̂, represent the predicted cell density distributions generated by each dynamical model, while the grey shaded regions, ρ, depict the observed cell density.
Table 6. Precision, recall and F1 for each cell type from pooled out-of-fold predictions under 5-fold stratified cross-validation. Support denotes the number of test cells aggregated across folds.
